title: Are America and China Condemned to Repeat History? Warnings of Great-Power Wars
content:
In his book 'The Coming Storm: Power, Conflict, and Warnings From History,' historian Odd Arne Westad explores the chilling parallels between current geopolitics and the era preceding World War I. He identifies several modern flash points, such as the Korean Peninsula, the South China Sea, and the Middle East, that could potentially ignite a global conflict. Westad warns that the great-power competition of a century ago still lurks within today's international relations. He argues that the world appears almost resigned to war, driven by a Freudian return of repressed imperial ambitions on a civilizational scale.

While some scholars argue that technological advancements have made war more manageable, Westad presents a radically different and darker nightmare. He notes a deeply held sense that great-power war is highly unlikely, leading to a dangerous refusal to reckon with the scale of potential destruction. The once ubiquitous existential terror of nuclear weapons has largely dissipated from popular consciousness. Instead, people are increasingly conditioned to view modern warfare as a matter of precision, control, and localization through smart weaponry and artificial intelligence. However, Westad insists there is almost no evidence that increased destructive potential will make war impossible.

The notion that the immense force of war can be completely controlled is a persistent and dangerous fantasy. As the French philosopher Simone Weil observed while analyzing Homer's 'Iliad,' human ambition is inevitably swept away and blinded by the very force it imagines it can handle. 'The Coming Storm' serves to remind readers of the enormity of unleashed military power and the catastrophic consequences of miscalculation. By assessing how the great powers of a hundred years ago went so wrong, the book offers an antidote to the paralysis of imagination. It ultimately challenges leaders to break the cycle of history and avoid the next devastating conflagration.
